answer:
**In "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" (Chapter 11 of *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*), Allama Iqbal depicts a visionary encounter where angels bring him (symbolized as the Nightingale of Hijaz) to the Prophet Muhammad's radiant court.**[^1]

The Prophet praises Iqbal's poetic devotion and asks what gift he brings from the earthly realm.[^2] Iqbal directly addresses the Prophet ("O Presence!") with a lament about the modern world's spiritual barrenness: there is no true repose for humanity, and amid life's abundant flowers, no bud carries the scent of genuine loyalty.[^3] 

Yet, as his offering, Iqbal presents a crystal phial containing the blood of the martyrs of Tripoli—a symbol absent even from Heaven's light, reflecting the enduring honor of the Prophet's Ummah.[^4]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11 "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-c8ea02).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11 "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-ae07ba).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11 "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-14a317).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11 "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" [passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-c179f0).
